Recent · Last 24 hours
Custom agents · Built for Cascadia Airways in Copilot Studio
IROps Cascade Commander
Scans case files across Sabre AirOps + SharePoint for missing docs and retention-policy issues across 6 divisions.
Live · last sync 2 min ago
Gate Turnaround Optimizer
Routes incoming case intakes (address change, turn plans of probable cause) and pre-stages the case file + manager loop.
3 new intakes awaiting review
Passenger Disruption Comms
Handles PTO balance lookups + requests in Teams, routes manager approvals, and posts to Sabre.
2 approvals pending >48h
Baggage Misconnect Orchestrator
Monitors 30/60/90-day onboarding checklists and nudges managers with specific outstanding items.
2 new hires off-track at day 30

CASCADIA AIRWAYS · SYSTEM OPERATIONS CENTER
W400 Cascadia Airways Courthouse · 516 Third Avenue · Seattle, WA 98104
Discovery production · Network Operations
Confidential · Discovery

WITNESS STATEMENT — M. Ortega, neighbor (Pg. 42)

Flight: Cascadia Airways · Flight CAS1247 SEA-ORD · No. CAS1247 · 2026-04-22 · Statement taken: April 16, 2026 · By: Ramp Lead J. Huang #2217 · Location: SEA Ramp Tower 4

I, Miguel Angel Ortega-Ramirez, being duly sworn, state the following based on my personal observation.

My date of birth is October 14, 1968. I reside at 4220 35th Avenue SW, Apt. 3B, Seattle, WA 98126, directly across the street from the residence identified in this matter. I have lived at that address for eleven years. My cell phone is (206) 555-0193 and my Social Security number, which the detective took for the record, is 541-24-8874.

On the evening of Saturday, April 11, 2026, at approximately 10:45 PM, I was watering the flowers on my front balcony when I heard what sounded like glass breaking from the house across the street. I looked over and observed a person, later identified to me by Ramp Lead Huang as Daniel Reyes, climbing through what appeared to be the rear kitchen window.

At approximately 10:52 PM, I observed Mr. Reyes exit the front door of the residence carrying what looked like a backpack and a bag of assorted items. He got into a dark-colored sedan parked on 35th Avenue SW and drove north. I wrote down the license plate as best I could read it: WA BQR-1184.

Additional context — confidential informant reference (page 42):

Ramp Lead Huang then asked whether I had ever seen Mr. Reyes in the neighborhood before. I explained that my cousin, Raymundo Castillo, who was CI-2024-117 in the Greenbridge task-force investigation, had mentioned seeing someone matching this description near the property twice in March.

The detective also asked about my son, a 14-year-old student at Sea-Tac Airport vicinity, Eduardo Ortega (DOB 2011-08-22), who was home with me that evening and confirmed he heard the glass break.

I have read this statement and it is true and correct to the best of my knowledge.

_________________________________________
Miguel Angel Ortega-Ramirez, Passenger
Signed at Seattle, WA this 16th day of April, 2026.

Manifest Pg. 42 of 312 · Batch-stamped for Flight CAS1247 · Processed by IROps Cascade Copilot at 06:18 AM · dispatcher-review required for items flagged in red

Live page · 5 editors

Q2 Office Operations Review

A shared canvas for Director of Operations’s Office. Copilot keeps the numbers and narrative in sync with the source files — when the Sabre AirOps dashboard updates, this page does too.

Confidential · Internal
Generated by Copilot from Q2 Network Operations Metrics.xlsx · synced 3 min ago
1,247
SOC service requests ▲7%
612
PTO in Teams ▲4×
68%
Self-service
4.6/5
Client satisfaction ▲0.3

The story this quarter

SOC inbox volume is up 7%, but Dispatcher touch-time is down sharply — the PTO agent in Teams auto-handled 612 requests that would have been inbox tickets last quarter, and the IROps Cascade Commander surfaced 237 compliance exceptions (14 missing ATP-Dispatcher currencies, 62 missing handbook acknowledgments) that are now 82% remediated. CSAT up 0.3 to 4.6/5. The pattern worth watching: verification-letter turnaround. We generated 184 letters but 11 took >2 days — the Front Door intake rollout in Phase 2 should close that.

Copilot suggestion based on this page + your Board briefing deck
Want me to add a Top 3 Wins section pulled from the Q2 town hall deck? I'll mirror the deck so if it changes, this page updates.

Copilot summary
April ticket volume climbed 7% while resolution time dropped 0.4 days — rare to see both move the right direction in the same month. The PTO agent in Teams absorbed most of the incremental PTO requests, which is why Dispatcher touch-time fell. 90-day attrition is flat and Client satisfaction ticked up 2 points, though the response-rate dip (Qualtrics migration) deserves a footnote in any cabinet readout.

Cascadia Airways SOC Copilot roadmap — 24 weeks
DK

Cascadia Airways SOC Copilot roadmap · 24 weeks · ~$350K

Three phases. Phase 0+1 foundations + quick wins · Phase 2 scale self-service · Phase 3 proactive HR. Shared with SOC×IT AI operating council.

18,000
Employees
68%
Self-service
$350K
Total budget
3/3
Phases on track
Kickoff
Week 1
Phase 0+1 GA
Week 10
Phase 2 GA
Week 18 · now
Phase 3 GA
Week 24
Phase 0+1

Enablement + Quick Wins · Weeks 1–10

✓ Completed · Week 10
Scope

SOC×IT AI operating model, governance baseline, Teams entry point decision (dedicated Network Operations channel), document repos + retention owners named. Quick wins: IROps Cascade Commander (1A) + Passenger Disruption Comms in Teams (1B, Sabre via Aquera).

Use cases live
  • Employee File Validation & Retention (ATP currency, W-4, offer, DD, handbook, background, license)
  • PTO balance lookup + request + manager approval in Teams
  • Audit log flowing to Purview
Outcomes hit
  • ✓ 237 file exceptions found · 82% remediated
  • ✓ PTO approval turnaround 2.1 d → 4.2 h
  • ✓ Governance guardrails GA'd
Investment

$135K total (weeks 1–10)

$75K partner-funding potential

Phase 2

Scale Turn-Plan Intake + Reduce Turn Plan Backlog · Weeks 10–18

● In progress · GA Week 18
Scope

Gate Turnaround Optimizer — structured intake & routing for Turn plan submissions, starting with scheduled-ops referrals and turn plan recommendations. Filing Manual RAG over FAR citations, ops standards, and internal ops memos with confidence gating + supervisor Dispatcher escalation.

Use cases piloting
  • Front Door intake for 1–2 request types
  • Handbook / policy RAG with citations
  • "Do it for me" address change + certified turn plan generation
Outcomes target
  • SOC inbox auto-handled ≥ 68%
  • Verification-letter TAT < 1 day
  • Client satisfaction ≥ 4.6 / 5
Investment

$110K (weeks 10–18)

Funding: TBD

Phase 3

Proactive / Predictive Operations · Weeks 18–24

◷ Planned · GA Week 24
Scope

One proactive workflow live: Onboarding nudges + manager 30/60/90 checklist reminders, event-triggered from hire date. Operational monitoring + governance cadence formalized with SOC×IT council.

Use cases target
  • Baggage Misconnect Orchestrator — 30/60/90 manager reminders
  • Off-track checklist detection + specific item nudges
  • Network Operations pilot → all 6 divisions
  • Quarterly AI governance cadence
Outcomes target
  • Checklist completion at 60d ≥ 85%
  • Early-attrition (90d) ↓ 2 pts
  • First predictive workflow at GA
Investment

$125K (weeks 18–24)

Funding: TBD

Governance

SOC×IT AI council meets biweekly. Purview auto-labels 100% of prompts. Data boundaries dispatcher/staff/crew-worker enforced; FAR 117 (Crew Duty) applies to crew-worker records. Every agent requires human-in-the-loop approval before write actions.

Risks watched

Sabre connector decision (Aquera vs direct API), license utilization (rebalance quarterly), Client satisfaction response rate during Qualtrics migration, Phase 2/3 funding confirmation.

Next check-in

Week 18 Cabinet readout. Executive Readout Agent is drafting the one-pager now.

Copilot recovery plans ranked

A
Route around ORD via MDW
recommended net pax delay −47% vs. do-nothing
  • Divert CAS1247 SEA→ORD → SEA→MDW · bus bridge ORD↔MDW (45 min, 184 pax)
  • Swap tails: N8421CA (737-800) ↔ N6152CA (737-900ER) at MDW to rebalance MDW→DEN leg
  • Deadhead FO Martinez SEA→DEN on CAS0517 to cover Pairing P4481 expiration
  • Cancel CAS1318 ORD→SEA (overnight tail rest) · 142 pax rebooked to CAS1322 + CAS1402

06:0006:3007:0007:3008:0008:3009:0009:3010:00
06:00 PT · Quiet SOC
Overnight shift still on console. SEA wx clear (210/12). KORD convective cell building west of the airport on CWA radar but no FAA action yet.
Decision trail ▸ red · disruption · 🧠 Copilot action · ✓ dispatcher decision · 🏁 outcome
Score: — · Pax saved: 0 · Cost avoided: $0K 0:00 / 0:40
CASE STUDY
CYVR diversion · 04/16 · 23:42 PT
CAS2201 OGG→SEA · transpac return · weather-deviation recovery · 2 tails, 186 pax · 1h 47m total disruption
186
pax exposed
172
pax on-time rebook
$42K
direct cost
$184K
EU261 avoided
✓
curfew preserved
9 min
Copilot-to-decision

What happened

CAS2201 OGG→SEA (B737-800, tail N8212CA, 172 pax) encountered convective cells 180 nm SW of SEA at 22:48 PT. Captain requested diversion; the CYVR (Vancouver) option was live. Problem: a KSEA 01:00 noise-abatement curfew was closing in, and the aircraft was scheduled to turn on a morning CAS4412 SEA→YVR the next day — which meant diverting OUT of SEA was a positioning nightmare.

Copilot's call (22:54)

IROps Cascade Commander ran 96 network sims in 64 s. Three options ranked:

  • Plan A ✓ recommended — Divert to CYVR (CAS2201) + position empty CAS4418 SEA→YVR at 04:30 next morning to pick up the stranded tail for its YVR→SEA rotation. 172 of 186 pax on-time via cross-hub rebook. Curfew preserved.
  • Plan B — Hold in the air for 25 min waiting on wx pass. Risk: fuel buffer; risk: curfew breach if hold extends.
  • Plan C — Divert to PDX. Cleanest aircraft positioning but 186 pax misconnect and $184K EU261 exposure (European-origin OGG pax).

What the dispatcher did (23:03)

Dispatcher K. Thompson accepted Plan A at 23:03 (9 minutes after the first alert). Copilot had pre-drafted:

  • Pax comms · 172 rebook notifications in EN/FR (YVR is bilingual) — tone-reviewed + approved at 23:08
  • Crew legality memo · Capt. Reyes + FO Yang on P4488 stayed legal under §c with a 10 h hotel overnight at YVR
  • Customs pre-clear for the unscheduled YVR arrival · completed through ARINC Direct
  • CAS4418 positioning flight dispatched at 04:30 next morning · rebalanced the rotation

What we learned

  • Copilot's "airport-fit" feature caught that CYVR + cross-hub positioning preserves both the curfew AND the next-day YVR rotation. A human analyst would likely have picked Plan C (cleaner positioning) under time pressure.
  • EU261 exposure math was a tie-breaker. The agent pulled PNR origins in 1.2 s — would have taken a dispatcher ~8 min to check manually.
  • One late issue: 4 special-meal pax weren't re-catered at YVR (contract handler limitation). We've since added \"special-meal feasibility\" as a Plan-B constraint in the Foundry scoring model.

Playback the full trail

Every step is in the Purview audit — original ACARS weather data, CWA radar snapshot, Copilot prompt + 96 sims, Dispatcher accept event, all 172 pax comms sent. Export is a one-click PDF for the FAA post-event review.

Live AI actions · last 5 min every decision logged to Purview · traceable to the source signal

ASSESSMENT · READY

Hi Sarah — I see the B12 alert. Ready to work through this with you.

Active disruption detected: Gate B12 jet bridge failure, Flight CAS1247 A320 SEA→MDW, departing in 45 minutes. I can generate ranked response options — but I'll reason before I recommend. Tell me the situation in your own words and what you're trying to protect.

DESCRIBE THE INCIDENT

What's the situation — and what are you optimizing for?

Fill with the example above
The AI will generate ranked response options. You can refine results using priorities and constraints.
Jet bridge at Gate B12 is out of service for an A320 departing in 45 minutes. I want to minimize passenger disruption.
SYSTEM ANALYSIS

Here's my understanding of the situation:

  • Jet bridge outage at Gate B12
  • A320 aircraft · narrow-body · 178 pax booked
  • Departure in ~45 minutes (STD 15:17 UTC)
  • Primary goal: minimize passenger disruption

I need one more detail to proceed.

What's the jet bridge MX ETA?
Chat explains existing options only.
Aircraft: Narrow-body · MX ETA: 15–60 min
RECOMMENDED OPTIONS

Based on the information provided, here are the best response options.

Option 1 ✓ Recommended
Reassign to Gate C14
Gate B12 moving to Gate C14 · minimal downstream conflicts · adequate walking distance
Cascadia Airways · No connections affected
Ops Impact
Low
Passenger
Low
Risk
Medium
Time to execute
Fast
Option 2
Reassign to Gate D6
Alternative terminal with adequate capacity · longer walk, second-choice compatibility
Cascadia Airways · 4 short connections at risk
Ops Impact
Low
Passenger
Medium
Risk
Low
Time to execute
Medium
Option 3
Hold at B12 · use bus-gate stairs
Stay at B12, deplane / enplane via air-stairs and ramp bus · preserves schedule if MX ETA < 20m
Cascadia Airways · WCHR/UMNR handling manual
Ops Impact
Medium
Passenger

Risk

Time to execute

Chat explains existing options only.
Selected Option 1 — Reassign to Gate C14
OPTION REVIEW

Here's a closer look at this option and why it was recommended.

Option 1 — Gate C14

Gate B12 moving to Gate C14

Gate C14 offers the best balance between operational feasibility and passenger experience. It minimizes disruption to existing gate assignments while maintaining acceptable walking distances.

Airline Impact

Cascadia Airways — No connections affected.

Key Assumptions
  • Gate C14 has compatible ground-support equipment available
  • Ramp crew can be repositioned within 15 minutes
  • No inbound delay expected for next scheduled flight at C14
  • Walking distance increase: approximately 200 meters
Risks & Trade-offs
  • Potential downstream conflict if B12 repair takes longer than expected
  • Slight increase in passenger walking distance
Important
Selecting an option does not apply system changes. Human-in-the-loop by design — execution still requires the checklist on the next step.
Selected Option 1 — Reassign to Gate C14
EXECUTION CHECKLIST

Coordinate across Airport Ops and Airline Ops. Check items as completed — every check writes back to Sabre + Dataverse + Purview.

Airport Operations
Airline Coordination
Watch-Outs
▸ Downstream gate conflicts
▸ Passenger walking distance · WCHR/UMNR accommodations
▸ Repair duration uncertainty — re-run advisor if MX ETA shifts
Roles, not names — can be mapped to airport/airline SOPs for deployment.
